text: The patient signed the informed consent.
We present the video of the first step, U-RATS right lower sleeve lobectomy.

This was the key for the plan management, the ability of the team to successfully limit the resection on the right side to lower lobectomy, closely related to the real invasion of the right carcinoma.
The patient was intubated into the left lung, then placed in lateral decubitus on the left side, as in any intercostal uniportal access into the thorax.
Like in every robotic thoracic surgery, the instruments for VATS and open thoracic surgery were prepared in the operating room as backup.
The robot was docked on the backside of the patient with active arms number 2, 3 and 4, and arm number 1 cancelled.
A 4 cm intercostal incision was placed on the right 7 intercostal space between anterior and middle axillary lines.
Intratorrassic inspection was completely performed.
Eurot's right lower sleeve lobectomy was performed.
The middle lobe was preserved by anastomosing the middle lobe bronchus to the bronchus intermedius using a continuous barbed suture.
Systematic nodal dissection was mandatory and it was performed in stations 12, 11, 10, 7, 8 and 9, 4R and 2R.
The patient was discharged on the fourth postoperative day after an uneventful course.

The pathological result showed a 3 cm polypoid squamous cell carcinoma, poorly differentiated, with implantation base of 2 cm presenting bronchial wall infiltration into the muscular layer and peribronchial connective tissue, but respecting the underlying lung.
with lymphatic and vascular invasion.
All lymph nodes were negative for malignancy.
The pathological stage was 1A3.
While watching the bronchial anastomosis, we will discuss the advantages of this bilateral URAT sleeve approach.
Bilateral synchronous primary lung cancers are uncommon and pose diagnostic, staging, and therapeutic challenges, especially when lung function is poor, like in the reported case.
The optimal personalized management of our patient raised a number of benchmark strategies.
Firstly, in this case, the diagnosis was easily obtained by bronchoscopic biopsies because both cancers presented with endobronchial components.
The fact that both originated in the bronchial mucosa favored the diagnosis of two primary squamous cell carcinomas, and they were classified as such.
Secondly, once the metastatic disease was ruled out, the best local treatment had to be decided, especially considering the first therapeutic plan offered at another hospital.
A bilateral sequential two stages surgery, not simultaneous because of limited respiratory reserve, was proposed for radical resection of both malignancies and reduction of hyperinflated lungs.
Bilateral sleeve lung resections are rare.
Only a few cases have been performed with at least one side by thoracotomy.
In the reported case, the newest, most modern, and most precise minimally invasive surgical technique was used, uniportal robotic assisted thoracic surgery.
To our knowledge, this is the first case ever reported of bilateral ureth sleeve lobectomies for simultaneous lung cancers.
Thirdly, the best sequence of the operations had to be determined.
Priority was given to the right-sided tumor because it caused sputum retention and risk of infection and to avoid compromise of the left lung.

And fourthly, alternative treatments were discussed in the multidisciplinary team meeting, like endobronchial resection of one or both tumors, brachytherapy, external radiotherapy, and their combination.

But a meticulous assessment of the CT scan indicated that both lesions could be completely resected by lung sparing surgery, and bilateral sleeve lobectomy was considered the best option for this patient.

The two-step sequential surgery achieved complete resection of both cancers, provided the most accurate staging, and the patient benefited of fast recovery and the possibility for early start of systemic therapy after a gain lung function following two lobectomies.
